content:
{
  "title": "Cross-Lingual Prompt Composition via Interlingua Anchoring for Multilingual LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models often struggle with tasks in low-resource languages due to limited training data and suboptimal prompt transfer from high-resource languages. Current prompting methods insufficiently bridge the semantic gap across languages, leading to degraded performance on multilingual benchmarks.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM performance in low-resource and multilingual contexts is critical for equitable access to language technologies. Existing prompt engineering largely assumes monolingual or high-resource settings; new methods are required to unlock multilingual capabilities, especially as LLMs are increasingly deployed globally.",
  "method": "We propose Cross-Lingual Prompt Composition (CLPC), a method that constructs prompts in low-resource target languages by first generating task prompts in multiple high-resource languages, translating them into an interlingua semantic representation using a multilingual encoder, and then decoding back to the target language prompt. This approach leverages semantic anchoring to reduce translation artifacts and preserve task intent. CLPC can be applied in-context or as an external prompt generator, and integrates with existing LLMs without additional fine-tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CLPC on the XTREME multilingual benchmark with a focus on low-resource languages (e.g., Swahili, Urdu). Baselines include direct translation of English prompts and multilingual prompt ensembling. Metrics will be task-appropriate (accuracy for classification, F1 for QA). The key experiment is to compare LLM zero-shot and few-shot task performance using CLPC prompts versus the strongest prompt translation baseline."
}
